BILLY / PRONOVOSTDIRECTOR OF TECHNOLOGY · PRODUCT + SOFTWARE BUILDER · DESIGNER

Small tools.
Big clues.

The fastest way to explain how I think is to let you try it.

RUN THREE TINY EXPERIMENTS ↓
LUMI / HERMES•WETHEAIS•EQUA•CEQR•ELUMRI•TOCIN

PRODUCT LAB / 001—003

No metaphor. Just working ideas.

001TRADE-OFF TUNERPurpose: make a fuzzy preference discussable.
Clear enough to trust. Strange enough to remember.
002DECISION SORTERPurpose: expose who a choice is serving.

A clever feature adds delight, but makes the main action harder to find. What moves first?

WAITING FOR A PRIORITY

Pick one. The answer is less important than making the trade-off visible.

003CONTINUITY CHECKPurpose: keep useful context without keeping everything.
Which notes should travel to the next session?
0 useful notes packed.

BUILT, NOT DISPLAYED

The experiments point back to real work.

Lumi / Hermes
continuity, memory, human–AI practice
WeTheAIs
public ideas about people building with AI
Equa
visible reasoning inside a decision tool
Ceqr + Tocin
small products with deliberately bounded jobs

PERSON.HTML

About the person running the lab.

Billy Pronovost is Director of Technology at Pendleton. He builds software and products, designs systems, and leads the work that has no tidy owner yet.

Before that: labor-union communications. It left a permanent bias toward plain language, real stakes, and tools that give people more agency—not less.

Current lab supplies: too many prototypes, exactly enough curiosity, and one strong opinion that every serious thing can still contain a small surprise.
